exercises.example_sentence: 父はまだ会社にいます。
exercises.translation: My dad is still at the office.
wrong_choice_explanations: もう means 'already,' suggesting dad has finished at the office and gone — the opposite of the sentence, which says he hasn't come home yet.
また means 'again,' but the sentence is about a single ongoing stay at the office, not a repeated visit.
ずっと means 'the whole time / continuously' and would say he has been at the office nonstop. The sentence is describing the present situation (he's still there now), not the entire duration up to now.
exercises.hiragana_full: ちちはまだかいしゃにいます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 父はまだオフィスで仕事をしています。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ちちはまだおふぃすでしごとをしています。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 今、父は会社から帰ってきていません。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: いま、ちちはかいしゃからかえってきていません。

exercises.example_sentence: 宿題はまだ終わっていません。
exercises.translation: I haven't finished my homework yet.
wrong_choice_explanations: もう with a negative means 'no longer / not anymore,' so 'もう終わっていません' would suggest the homework went from finished back to unfinished — the sentence simply says it hasn't reached completion the first time.
ぜんぜん means 'not at all,' so 'ぜんぜん終わっていません' would say no progress has been made. The sentence only says the work isn't done yet — the speaker may have done plenty of it.
あまり with a negative means 'not much / not very,' so 'あまり終わっていません' would say 'not much is done.' That speaks to the amount of progress, not to the simple fact that it isn't finished yet.
exercises.hiragana_full: しゅくだいはまだおわっていません。

audio_alternatives.kanji: まだ宿題が終わっていないんです。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: まだしゅくだいがおわっていないんです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 宿題はあと半分くらい残っています。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: しゅくだいはあとはんぶんくらいのこっています。

exercises.translation: My little sister is still only five.
wrong_choice_explanations: もう means 'already,' so 'もう五歳' would say 'already five' — that frames being five as a milestone reached, not as the speaker's point that she is still young.
やっと means 'finally / at long last,' so 'やっと五歳' would say she has finally turned five after a wait. The sentence is treating five as a young age, not as a long-anticipated milestone.
ちょうど means 'exactly,' so 'ちょうど五歳' would precisely state her age. The sentence is doing more than stating the number — it is emphasizing that she is still very young.
exercises.hiragana_full: いもうとはまだごさいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 妹はまだ五歳になったばかりだ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: いもうとはまだごさいになったばかりだ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 妹は今年五歳で、まだ小学校に入っていません。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: いもうとはことしごさいで、まだしょうがっこうにはいっていません。

exercises.example_sentence: 雨はまだ降っています。
wrong_choice_explanations: もう means 'already,' so 'もう降っています' would suggest the rain has already started — but the sentence is about rain that has been going and hasn't stopped, not rain just beginning.
また means 'again,' which would say the rain has started up a second time. The sentence is describing the same single stretch of rain still in progress, not a repeat shower.

ずっと means 'the whole time / continuously,' so it would describe rain going on uninterrupted across a long stretch. The sentence is focused on the rain still being underway right now, not on its full duration.
exercises.hiragana_full: あめはまだふっています。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 外ではまだ雨が降り続いている。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: そとではまだあめがふりつづいている。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 雨はやんでいないので、出かけられません。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あめはやんでいないので、でかけられません。

exercises.example_sentence: 彼からの返事はまだ来ません。
wrong_choice_explanations: もう with a negative means 'no longer,' so 'もう来ません' would mean 'his reply isn't coming anymore' — that assumes it once was coming, while the sentence is about a reply that simply has not arrived yet.
ぜんぜん means 'not at all,' so 'ぜんぜん来ません' would say no contact has come whatsoever. The sentence only says the reply hasn't arrived yet — it isn't passing judgment on the whole relationship.
あまり with a negative means 'not very,' but a reply either comes or it doesn't — 'not very come' doesn't fit; the sentence is about a single missing message, not a frequency.
exercises.hiragana_full: かれからのへんじはまだきません。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 彼からの連絡はまだ届いていない。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: かれからのれんらくはまだとどいていない。

audio_alternatives.kanji: メッセージを送ったが、彼はまだ返信してくれない。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: めっせーじをおくったが、かれはまだへんしんしてくれない。

exercises.example_sentence: 朝ご飯はまだ食べていません。
wrong_choice_explanations: もう with a negative means 'no longer,' so 'もう食べていません' would say 'I no longer eat breakfast,' implying a habit the speaker dropped — the sentence is about today's breakfast not having happened yet.
ぜんぜん means 'not at all,' so 'ぜんぜん食べていません' would say no food at all has been eaten. The sentence is just talking about breakfast not having happened — the speaker might have eaten other things.

あまり with a negative means 'not very,' so 'あまり食べていません' would say 'I haven't eaten much.' The sentence is making a yes/no point about not having eaten breakfast at all, not commenting on a small portion.
exercises.hiragana_full: あさごはんはまだたべていません。

audio_alternatives.kanji: まだ朝ごはんを食べていないんです。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: まだあさごはんをたべていないんです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 朝食はこれから食べる予定です。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ちょうしょくはこれからたべるよていです。

exercises.example_sentence: このパンはまだ温かいです。
wrong_choice_explanations: もう means 'already,' so 'もう温かい' would say the bread has just heated up — but the sentence is about freshly baked bread that hasn't cooled yet, not bread that has just gotten warm.

ずっと means 'the whole time,' so 'ずっと温かい' would say the bread has been warm all along. The sentence focuses on warmth lingering up to this moment from when it was baked, not on a constant temperature across a long span.
とても means 'very,' so 'とても温かい' just intensifies how warm it is. The sentence is making a time-related point (it hasn't cooled down yet), not a comment on degree.
exercises.hiragana_full: このぱんはまだあたたかいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: このパンは焼きたてで、まだ温かい。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: このぱんはやきたてで、まだあたたかい。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 焼いたばかりのパンなので、冷めていません。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: やいたばかりのぱんなので、さめていません。

exercises.example_sentence: 試験の結果はまだ分かりません。
exercises.translation: The exam results aren't in yet.
wrong_choice_explanations: もう with a negative means 'no longer,' so 'もう分かりません' would say 'I don't understand anymore,' implying the speaker used to know — the sentence is about results that simply haven't been released yet.
ぜんぜん means 'not at all,' so 'ぜんぜん分かりません' would say the speaker has no idea about the topic in general. The sentence is making a narrower point — the results are pending, not unknowable.
あまり with a negative means 'not very,' so 'あまり分かりません' would say 'I don't really understand.' The sentence is about timing — the news hasn't arrived — not a partial understanding of what is known.
exercises.hiragana_full: しけんのけっかはまだわかりません。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 試験の結果はまだ発表されていない。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: しけんのけっかはまだはっぴょうされていない。

audio_alternatives.kanji: テストの点数はそのうち分かるでしょう。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: てすとのてんすうはそのうちわかるでしょう。

exercises.example_sentence: 駅まではまだ遠いです。
exercises.translation: It's still a long way to the station.
wrong_choice_explanations: もう means 'already,' so 'もう遠いです' would oddly say 'it's already far,' as though the distance suddenly appeared — the sentence is about distance the speaker has yet to cover.

ずっと means 'far and away' or 'continuously,' so 'ずっと遠い' would emphasize that the distance is way more than expected. The sentence is giving a time-based update — there's still a stretch left to walk — not exaggerating the scale.
とても means 'very,' just intensifying how far it is. The sentence is reporting that distance remains from the current point, not commenting on how far the total is.
exercises.hiragana_full: えきまではまだとおいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 駅まではまだかなり距離があります。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: えきまではまだかなりきょりがあります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: ここから駅まで歩くと、もう少し時間がかかります。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ここからえきまであるくと、もうすこしじかんがかかります。

wrong_choice_explanations: もう means 'already,' so 'もう寝ています' would say 'mom is already asleep,' meaning she has just gone to bed — but the sentence is about her not yet being awake in the morning.
また means 'again,' so 'また寝ています' would say she's sleeping again after waking once. The sentence describes the same continuous sleep from the night, not a second nap.
ずっと means 'the whole time,' so 'ずっと寝ています' would emphasize how long she has been sleeping. The sentence simply notes she hasn't gotten up yet, not the total duration of her sleep.
exercises.hiragana_full: はははまだねています。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 母はまだ起きていない。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: はははまだおきていない。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 今、母は布団の中で眠っているところだ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: いま、はははふとんのなかでねむっているところだ。
